sentences.text: The next step after that.
Right.
As I said, I enjoyed that school tremendously.
It was one of the highlights of my life, that and my visit in Berlin with my aunt and uncle.
And I was looking forward to entering into the next phase of my education.
And that-- that was the school really about two blocks away from there.
It held a lot of fascination for me whenever I walked by.
I thought, oh, what is going on here?
You know, here's an 8, 9, 10 year old dreaming about being a teenager.
And there's a German word for a young girl, girl teenager.
And it's called, [NON-ENGLISH].
It's very funny, because it really means baked fish.
And when you translate it, it makes no sense at all.
Excuse me.
And I saw these young girls, you know on the-- or heared-- or heard them frolicking around in the schoolyard.
There was a very high fence there.
And I was looking forward to the choice of getting older and-- and so on and so forth.
And you know how it is when you're like 8, 9, 10 years old. 13, 14-- that, oh, they can do such wonderful things already that you want to be able to do, too.
So I was looking forward to attending there.
And I-- I was a good student.

And in Germany, the-- the system was that at the end of-- close to the end of your first four years of public school, you take really a battery of tests verbal and-- verbal and written, mostly written, and to see if you can go on to the next step, which is called, realschule.
I really can't say what that is exactly.
It's like a combination-- or hochschuler.
It's a combination of junior high and high school is really what that is, the next step.
Realschule.
R-E-A-L.
Schule-- S-C-H-U-L-E.
Hochschuler.
H-O-C-H and Schuler.
So this was called the Helena Lunger Uber Hochschuler.
Meaning, it had been named after a woman.
I don't know who she was.
I never did pay much attention to that.
But that school was named after her.
It had-- also, it had another name.
And that was Hansa.
H-A-N-S-A.
Hansahochschuler.
Hansa is a word that means-- that was a league of Hanseatic cities.
So Hansa-- everything having to do with Hamburg, Bremen, Lubeck.
Those were the Hanseatic cities.
I think even Amsterdam was a Hanseatic city.
It was an allegiance of port cities during the late middle ages that all banded together to protect themselves.
And that was the Hanseatic League.
So hansa relates to that.
And so I-- I-- was having great expectations of attending that school.
And I passed my tests.
And I was admitted to the school.
And-- excuse me.
At that point in time, it was Easter of 1934.
And the Nazis had been in-- had been-- the government, legal government of Germany for over a year.
They what-- what happened at that school was a complete and total not only disappointment, but shock.
First of all, I was-- there were several from my first four year class, several other girls who also entered that school.
None of them were in the same class.
The class I was put in was all strangers and were all gentiles, no Jewish, not even the half-Jewish girl that also wound up going there in another class.
And they had come from schools all over the city I guess.
And I immediately encountered problems.
The teacher was a Miss.
Arnold.
And Miss.
Arnold was evidently a very ardent Hitler fan, admirer.
She was actually in love with him.
She had a large picture of Hitler in the classroom, which was not required.
And-- excuse me-- she would bring fresh flowers every day, a small bouquet of violets or something every day to stick behind the frame for the Fuhrer.
And so it goes without saying that you can imagine what this woman was made of and what her-- what her state of mind was.
It was unfortunate, extremely unfortunate, for me, because not every teacher in that school was anything like that.
However, as I much later found out, the principle of that school wanted by all means to push out the Jewish students, the Jewish pupils and to 'purify it,' quote, unquote.
And so I guess they used whatever means they could.
It-- it was-- it was an over-ambitious scheme of his, because the government had required that by such and such year and such and such time this should occur.
But he was going to push it and make it happen sooner.
So at that point in time, the regime had been in place well over a year, as I mentioned.
And by that point in time, they had already instituted a whole lot of anti-Semitic measures, laws enacted, and so forth.
One of the things that they required in every school is an hour of instruction on National Socialism.
Now, that's not what it was called.
And I can't recall what it was called.
But that's what it basically was.
And so what happened was that when I started there and this class was taking place, and of course, the teacher was this woman-- although, we had other teachers.
For English, we had another teacher, for French we-- no, I started with English.
So French didn't come until later.
For English, we had another teacher.
For history, we had another teacher.
For Geometry, we had another teacher.
But this was our so-called homeroom teacher, Miss.
Arnold.
Fraulein Arnold.
And so she would start the class.
And there was-- of course, you said, how did we know?
Or, how-- how, you know, things were done?
Well, you either knew it by-- you knew it by instinct.
Or, you knew enough to know what was going on and what the attempts were and what the goals were of the Nazis.
And the class was starting.
And there had been notices made whether the few remaining Jewish pupils in that school would remain in class while this instruction was taking place.
So the first few times, I was there.
Because I couldn't leave.
I mean, I would have been-- I would have been punished if I had left on my own.
And I-- no decision had been made that I should not be there.
So she started to talk about this and that and the glory of Germany and how Hitler was going to, you know, restore everything to its glory and to the German people, and then a whole bunch of stuff.

And then, of course, you had to-- you had to start on the Jews to infuse the children-- and we were then 10 years old, 10 to 10 and 1/2-- to infuse them with this un-- unlimited hatred by making Jews appear as such outrageously ugly and evil bogeymen.
So she was-- they were talking about the racial characteristics, blonde hair, blue eyes.
You know, Jews are dark and this and that and the other thing.
And I was sitting there.
And I had blond hair and blue eyes.
And one of the girls in the class actually raised her hand-- which was-- it was either courageous or dumb-- and asked Miss.
Arnold, well, how is it that that isn't always true?
Because here, Thea Bloch-- that was me-- is sitting here.
And she is Jewish, and she has blond hair and blue eyes.
And I have dark hair and dark eyes.
And a lot of others have as well dark hair and dark eyes.
And the woman was really dumbstruck.
She started to stutter a little bit, the teacher.
And she, well, well, you have to realize when you look at Thea's hair very carefully, you will-- you will see that there is like dark shades underneath.
My hair wasn't bleached.
So it was natural.
And there were no dark shades underneath.
But this is how she attempted to explain it away.
And then, your-- your dark hair really have some golden light highlights when you look real close.
All-- all of you who have dark hair, you have some golden light highlights.
And that's how she explained it away.
And this is the kind of garbage, you know, that was fed to-- to the German children at that time.
These are the children then who were sucked into the BDM, which was the girl's Hitler-- part of the Hitler Youth and the Hitler Youth, which was the boy's part of the Hitler Youth.
And they were constantly subjected to what those evil, ugly, sly, despicable Jews are up to.
And that you must have nothing-- that good Germans, have nothing to do with them.
And those are the people that when the war started, they were like 20, 21 years old.
And this is what they had been fed since the age of 10.
And I don't know how much blame you can really attach to that.
Those are the things that I always thought about that I still think about today.
Also, they were taught that-- and I heard this first-hand from them.
Because there was some who was still confiding in me.
That if they hear their parents-- now, this was not in school.
This was if they were in the Hitler Youth.
And they had their meetings.
And they had their evenings.
And they did their marching and their singing, their camaraderie like stuff, which is fun, you know?
I even hoped that-- I even kind of thought sometimes I'd like to be in it myself, except for this Jewish stuff.
That's how they got drawn in.
That if their parents expressed themselves in saying something about Hitler or any of the top Nazis, that they are to report that as good German children.
They are to report that.
And it was not an unusual thing that people would heard the doorbell ring in the middle of the night and were picked up because of their children.
So those are some of the things that happened there.
And what also happened was that there came the break period where we were in between classes.
And we were out in the schoolyard.
And nobody, but nobody played with me.
And I was standing there alone.
And coming from the other school, it was-- it was such a shock and such a trauma for me.
Excuse me.
It was such a trauma for me then.
[DRINKS] I had a hard time dealing with it.
But I did not tell my parents about it at home.
I was ashamed or something.
I don't know.
At the end of the year-- excuse me.
At the end of the year, my parents decided nevertheless that I needed-- that I should not any longer attend that school.
My mother must have found out somehow.
And I transferred to the Jewish Girl's School in Hamburg.
I would like to mention one thing.
And that is that my cousin, who was one of two-- one of twins and he was a boy and there was a girl.
They were-- they were twins, what you those-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unequal.
Whats the word I'm trying to-- they were--
They were fraternal twins.
That's not the worth either, but whatever.
So one was a boy, one was a girl.
I played with them frequently.
I was in their home a lot.
And they had a-- what we would consider a cooperative apartment-- which they had purchased in 1921 and which they had to move out of which was being purified, which they had to move out of in 1938.
Just give up, they got no money for it.
But my cousin with whom I played frequently, both he and his sister, in [NON-ENGLISH], he had a very good friend.
His best friend was Walter Burgerman.
Walter Burgerman, Walter Burgerman a nice young boy about the same age as Hans Joachin.
And they frequently played.
And the parents were good friends.
And they knew each other since maybe they were 4 or 5 years old.
And they were still very good friends when they were 10, 11, and 12.
When he was 13, my cousin became bar mitzvah.
And there was a-- a ceremony in the temple.
And then there was a little party in the apartment.
That was-- then, were still in co-op apartment.
And this was-- I was 11 years old, so it was 1935.
And so that was two years into the Nazi government.

So this Walter Burgerman, whom he was seeing very, very rarely at that point in time, and who was not there, rang the bell in his Hitler Youth uniform, in his Hitler Youth uniform, which you know was a swastika on the arm and a brown shirt and the triangle, the tie.
And he wanted to congratulate my cousin.
And I see him there-- as I'm sitting here today-- I see him reaching out his hand to my cousin.
[SOBS] And he's wanted to congratulate him.
It was a stranger.
It was the strangest, the strangest situation.
But the kid-- he was a kid.
They were 13 years old.
I mean, what did he know about hating this particular person, because he was a Jew?
So this is one of the things that I feel that is kind of significant to know.
That just because they were Germans, they weren't all immediately converted to this way of thinking.
They knew that they weren't to associate with Jewish Germans anymore.
But there he was, he came.
And there was another-- there was another little innocent situation when I was still in my first four years of school.
There was one girl with whom I was friendly who lived not far from us who went-- who was in the Hitler Youth at an early age.
And some of them went in there, because these were-- these were children-- these were only children.
And there were a lot of only-- only children, because the economy was so bad.
They had no brothers and sisters.
I had no brothers and sisters.
And they went there for camaraderie, and fun, and marching, and music, and singing.
And anyway this girl, too, had been inducted, had joined the BDM [GERMAN], which is the girls' part of the Hitler Youth-- was the girls' part of the Hitler Youth.
And she was a dark haired girl with long-- very dark hair-- with long braids, very husky.
And she came to school in uniform.
Because right after school, I guess she had a meeting.
And she said to me out of the clear blue sky as we were having a break in between classes-- we were peeling oranges over the wastebasket.
I still remember that.
I can even smell the orange.
They were jaffa oranges.
And she said to me, now, I want you to try on this uniform, because on you it looks-- it'll look so much better than on me.
I said, are you crazy?
I can't-- I don't want to try on this uniform.
You know, I-- you know, didn't-- I don't know what I said.
I really and truly did not want to try on her uniform.
But she pushed the jacket on me.
And she said, now, look at that.
That looks like-- in today's lingo what you would say, cool.
She really looks cool with this on.
And it looks so much better on her than on me.
Now, that's just an-- I mean, this is total innocence.
This is just a little incidence I wanted to-- wanted to mention.
Yes.
All right.
I-- in 1935-- by the time it was 1935.
And I was in enrolled then in the Jewish school.
And-- and again, the schools were named after the streets that they were on in the [GERMAN] Strasse.
And the [GERMAN] Strasse, it's like Caroline Street is really what it is.
And this school was run by Jews, staffed by Jews.
The children, many of whom went there of course, came off from observing-- mostly from observing homes, Orthodox.
There really was no Conservative.
There was Reform.
And there was Orthodox.
And the Reform was more Conservative, like Conservatism here.
So most of them, many of them were Orthodox.
And they had had Hebrew from the first year of school.
And here I was-- 11 years old, 11 and 1/2 almost.
And I had not had Hebrew, even though I was attending the temple instructions, I wasn't learning Hebrew there.
And where was I going to start?
And it was like, you know, I had so many years that were lost to me as far as Hebrew was concerned.
And I was expected to keep up.

What I did keep up was-- was the Hebrew songs we sang, which were what later became Israel, Palestinian, those kinds of songs that became very popular amongst young Jewish people, like and all that kind of thing.
And so that I could keep up with.
And I knew all of them.
But I couldn't keep up with the Torah reading.
I couldn't keep up with anything like that.
And so that put me in a bad situation right off.
Some of the teachers there I was very unhappy with.
Now, it was like going from the frying pan into the fire.
It was some anti-Semitism to a certain kind of anti-Jewish-- anti-Reformed Jewish feeling, which is one-- I was also attending a Jewish day camp in the summer.
And as somebody very succinctly put it at that time, you really don't belong to us.
And you don't belong to them.
And that was really almost true.
But I wasn't the only one.
So-- and one of my pals was the-- who was the same age-- was the daughter of the principal of that school.
Who kept a-- who kept a Kosher home, and who were Orthodox, but modern Orthodox and who along with me made fun of every one of the teachers at the school.
It was a very difficult adjustment.
It was a very difficult adjustment.
At that point in time, we had moved to the other address, which was actually much closer to that school.
That school was in an older part of the city.
And I walked there.
I never had any problem walking there.
I-- pretty soon, I made a very good friend, a girl who lived not far from there and who would often time come and pick me up.
And we would walk together.
She was a girl from, I think it was Magdeburg, a town a little south of Hamburg where they didn't have Jewish schools.
And they no longer wanted to have the Jewish children in the public schools.
So she had to come to Hamburg and be boarded with a Jewish family, and then went to school there.
Her name was Inge Fisser.
And Inge Fisser and I had a great time walking to school together.
We laughed our heads off.
We had-- so you see, as children then you block out certain things.
You don't think about the situation that you're in all the time.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we became very good friends.
As a matter of fact, we went downtown to a movie.
And it was something with Greta Garbo and Robert Taylor.
And actually, we were not even supposed to do that as Jewish girls.
I think the law had already been passed that you weren't supposed to go into the movie theaters.
And we sat there and loudly laughed like idiots, but like-- like young teenagers.
Nobody bothered us.
And the years there I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I really didn't care much for any of the teachers.
There were-- there was an occasional absence of this one or that one at which time, this Dr.
Jonas, who was the father, who was the principal, would then take over a class and teach a class.
And I thought he was a marvelous teacher.
So I enjoyed that.
I don't know what very much else I enjoyed there.
I really don't remember.
The last year, I had-- I did have a teacher.
And I was in another location, which had formally been a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a single family home, and then a Jewish school, a Jewish private school, a private Jewish girl's school.
And they took that over.
And so the older-- some of the older students would attend there.
And that's where I was going in 1938.

And when the-- when it happened that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a young Polish Jew had shot and killed a second or third rate-- not a second or third rate-- but a minor-- a minor official in the German embassy in Paris.
And, oh, outrage.
Outrage in the press.
This was not amongst Germans as such, but outrage in the press.
How dare a Jew kill a German.
And this is what then brought on what is-- what later became to-- came to be known as Kristallnacht.
The outrage over this act by this young Polish Jew.
And what happened had happened.
And the reason he did that-- at that point in time, they started to deport Jews who had come from Poland during this century.
And there were quite a few and had settled in Germany and made a good living there.
Their children were born there.
But they were from Poland.
I had several in my class in the Jewish school.
Excuse me.
One was very wealthy, the other one was very smart.
And, you know, and we had a great time.
And-- and they started to deport those Polish Jews to the Polish border.
At which point, Poland did not want to accept them and quickly took away their Polish citizenship, which they-- which they still had.
And they became stateless.
That was the worst of all evils, to be stateless.
And they were-- and they were hungry.
There was no-- there was nothing for them there.
They were like dumped into-- in-- they weren't concentration camps.
They were just dumped there.
They had nothing to eat.
They had no money.
They had nothing.
And so this young man, he was 17 years old, I guess had received a letter from his parents telling him in what desperate situation they are and to see if he could send them something.
And what he had in mind, I don't know.
But he went to the embassy with a gun in his hand.
Whether the gun went off by mistake-- he killed this minor German diplomat.
And that's what's set off the-- the happenings of Kristallnacht.
